items.q: What is the structure of a Shakespearean sonnet?
items.correct: Fourteen lines in iambic pentameter, structured as three quatrains and a final couplet, rhyming ABAB CDCD EFEF GG, often with a volta near the couplet.
items.misconception: A sonnet is any short love poem.
items.random_wrong: A sonnet must always be exactly ten lines long.
items.why: Students treat sonnet as a vague genre. It is a strict 14-line form with specific rhyme schemes — and sonnets need not be about love (think Hopkins's religious sonnets).

items.correct: The claim that judging a text by what the author 'intended' is fallacious — the text's meaning lies in the work itself, not the author's stated or guessed intentions.
items.misconception: It means authors can never make mistakes when writing.
items.random_wrong: It means it is wrong to write fiction.
items.why: Students assume 'author intent' settles meaning. Wimsatt and Beardsley argued it doesn't — meaning is a public property of the text, not a private fact about the author.

items.q: What did Romantic poets value in nature?
items.correct: Nature as a source of spiritual insight and emotional truth, accessed through imagination — not picturesque scenery for its own sake.
items.misconception: Romantic poets just liked to write about pretty landscapes.
items.random_wrong: Romantic poets believed nature was full of fairies and elves.
items.why: The Romantic engagement with nature was philosophical — it was a route to truth, the sublime and the self. Reducing it to 'pretty scenery' misses Wordsworth's epistemology entirely.

items.q: A turn or shift in a poem's argument is called:
items.why: Correct answer: volta. “caesura” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: sibilance. “plosive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In a Shakespearean sonnet, the volta typically appears at which line?
items.why: Exact answer: 9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
